title: V2 Analysis: 3-Front Attack on Domain Overfitting

what_we_tried.front_1: Dynamic Macro-to-Micro crop augmentation (35% prob, 300-600px, ≥2 textures)
what_we_tried.front_2: Semantic Cycle-Consistency via InverseProjector (256→4096) with gradient firewall
what_we_tried.front_3: Constrained Qwen LoRA (r=4, alpha=8) — 4x less capacity

what_worked.crop_augmentation: Eliminated val/test divergence. Test mIoU tracked val throughout training. In V1, test peaked at ep5 then collapsed -20%. In V2, test climbed continuously to 0.695 at ep15.
what_worked.constrained_lora: Prevented Qwen from memorizing ADE20K patterns. Phase 2 improved BOTH val and test (V1: Phase 2 improved val but destroyed test).
what_worked.cycle_consistency: InverseProjector converged fast (1.86→0.42). Confirms projector retains semantic information in 256-dim space.
what_worked.overall: Best test mIoU ever: 0.695 = 98% of ZS baseline. Val 0.704. No divergence.

discovery_1.title: Qwen's descriptions are excellent — the language side is NOT the bottleneck
discovery_1.detail: When Qwen generates exactly 2 descriptions, they are semantically perfect (mIoU=0.97). The problem is count control (77% predict k=6) and SAM's inability to use multiple channels.

discovery_2.title: Prompt count instruction is counterproductive
discovery_2.detail: Forcing 'exactly 2' in the prompt REDUCED mIoU (-0.035). More descriptions = more chances for Hungarian matching to find a good match. The '1 to 6' prompt with over-generation is actually optimal.

discovery_3.title: SAM3 has catastrophic positional bias (Slot 1 Addiction)
discovery_3.detail: Slot 1 gets 90.5% of pixels, Slot 2 gets exactly 0.0% — regardless of content, position swap, or isolated runs. SAM was pretrained for single-prompt and its cross-attention weights are hardwired to attend only to position 1. This is the FUNDAMENTAL architectural bottleneck preventing multi-texture segmentation.
discovery_3.impact: No amount of training, prompt engineering, or embedding quality improvement can fix this. The architecture must change.

discovery_4.title: The val mIoU (0.70) is achieved by one channel doing all the work
discovery_4.detail: The model learns to put the dominant texture in channel 1 and dustbin for the rest. For images with one large texture, this gives decent IoU. True multi-texture segmentation never happens.

v1_run4.approach: Direct 1-to-1, Qwen bypass P1, SAM r=32
v1_run4.weakness: Qwen LoRA overfitting, test collapsed to 0.544

v2_run5.approach: V1 + crop aug + cycle loss + LoRA r=4
v2_run5.weakness: SAM positional bias — only 1 channel active, multi-texture impossible

v3_planned.approach: V2 + Batch Multiplexing (each query in own slot 1)
v3_planned.expected: True multi-texture segmentation. Slot 2+ should activate.

critical_insight: The journey revealed a layered bottleneck: V1 found the LM gradient dominance problem (fixed by curriculum). V2 found the Qwen overfitting problem (fixed by crop aug + constrained LoRA). The positional bias experiments then revealed the DEEPEST bottleneck: SAM's architecture itself cannot handle multi-query prompts. V3's Batch Multiplexing is the surgical fix — give each query its own 'slot 1' by expanding into the batch dimension.
lessons_learned: Always test inference separately from val — teacher forcing hides architectural bugs
Extract and analyze the generated descriptions — the language quality was a red herring
Positional bias in pretrained models is real and devastating — always test with swapped inputs
Crop augmentation is cheap but powerful for domain generalization
Constrained LoRA (small r) prevents catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning
More descriptions can be better than fewer — let the matching algorithm select the best K
